invariant_definition: Release from bondage, but not a bare escape from existence. *Mokṣa* names the final state appropriate to what a school takes bondage to be: the nexus of *karma*, rebirth, embodiment, ignorance, misidentification, or estrangement from the Lord. It is therefore defined against adjacent terms such as *saṃsāra*, *bandha*, *apavarga*, and *kaivalya*. In the Mīmāṃsā background, the point is that no finite ritual act can simply manufacture *mokṣa* as a produced result; in the Vedāntic setting, the issue becomes what sort of cognition, devotion, surrender, or divine grace removes the condition that makes rebirth possible. The practical register is inseparable from the metaphysics: Advaita ties *mokṣa* to the removal of *avidyā* through *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana*; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) tie it to God-directed knowledge and devotion; Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism treats even ordinary *mokṣa* as lower than *prema*. What remains invariant is this: *mokṣa* is the non-returning condition in which the structures that sustain saṃsāric agency, enjoyment, and compulsory embodiment no longer bind.

per_school.definition: *Mokṣa* is *ajñāna-nivṛtti*, not a *kārya* produced by ritual action; since action itself belongs within the economy of ignorance, liberation depends on *jñāna* alone [[UpS I.11.15](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15); [NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)]. More exactly, *anyadṛṣṭi* is *avidyā*, and the destruction of that misapprehension through the sentence-meaning of the Upaniṣadic *mahāvākya* is what is called *mokṣa* [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. Accordingly, liberation is present at the very rise of self-knowledge: when the *ātmajñānāgni* burns the seed of saṃsāra without remainder, the knower is already *vimukta* [[IṢ 1.9](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jīvanmuktiḥ)].

Vedānta teaches release by making known the *jīva* as distinct from body, the *antaryāmin* Paramātman, the *upāsanā* of him, and the ensuing manifestation of the self together with an unsurpassed experience of Brahmanic bliss [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3); [VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. When *karma*-generated differentiations such as deva-, manuṣya-, and the like are removed, the self's own *svarūpa* remains a *jñānānandaika-guṇa* reality, beyond verbal objectification yet not annihilated [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)]. The practical stance toward this release is *prapatti*: the *akiñcana* who is *ananyagati* takes refuge at the Lord's feet as the sole shore beyond *bhavārṇava* [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

*Mokṣa* is not yielded by knowledge as an autonomous natural power; Viṣṇu himself gives knowledge to the ignorant, liberation to the knowers, and bliss to the liberated [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. The *jīva* never becomes essentially identical with Īśvara: even in liberation its consciousness and bliss remain as qualities similar to the Lord's, while *jīveśvara-bheda* stands firm by the force of śruti [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. The soteriological sequence is *vairāgya* -> *bhakti-dārḍhya* -> *upāsanā* -> *Viṣṇv-aparokṣya*, so liberation is inseparable from contemplative devotion [[Anuvy. 3.3.1](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1)]. Among the liberated there is enduring *taratamya*: those fit for release rejoice in graded degrees of excellence rather than in an undifferentiated sameness [[VTV 3.27](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.27)].

Because the *jīva* is related to Bhagavān by simultaneous *bheda* and *abheda* under his *acintya-śakti*, the *abheda-śāstras* are to be coordinated only after contradiction has been removed by affirming both difference from and non-difference from him [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once Bhagavān and the *māyā-mohita* jīva stand thus distinguished, his *bhajana* alone has the status of *abhidheya*, and *parama-prema* for Bhagavān is fixed as the *prayojana* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44); [§45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. Accordingly, even *sālokya*, *sārṣṭi*, *sāmīpya*, *sārūpya*, and *ekatva* are refused apart from *mat-sevana*, while pure *uttamā-bhakti* is unoverlaid by *jñāna* and *karma* [[BRS 1.1.11](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.11); [BRS 1.1.14](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.14); [BRS 1.1.16](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.16)].

The jīva is *jñāna-svarūpa*, atomic, a knower, and dependent on Hari, being fit for both conjunction with and disjunction from embodiment [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. Its bondage is beginningless conjunction with *māyā*, whereas its released condition is known by Bhagavān's grace; the same self is spoken of as bound, liberated, and devoted according to its condition [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. Hence the jīva is neither absolutely different from nor absolutely non-different from Śrī Puruṣottama, but an *aṃśa* understood as his dependent *śakti* [[VK 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The consummation sought is a *kṛpā-phala* beyond mere release, namely the *bhakti-rasa* of the worshipper in relation to the worshipped Lord [[DaŚ 10](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10)].

Brahman is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa* of the world as *sat-cit-ānanda*, and the jīva is truly Brahman's *aṃśa* rather than an adventitious superimposition [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4); [BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. With Brahman-knowledge and the cessation of *avidyā*, fitness for one's own *svarūpa* is accomplished for the attainment of Puruṣottama; when the soul is accepted as his own, *bhakti* functions as the necessary *sahakāri* for that attainment [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

*Mokṣa* is the exhaustion of *karma* and *kleśa*, and these in turn depend upon *vikalpa* and *prapañca*; when proliferative elaboration is stilled in *śūnyatā*, the whole bondage-series collapses [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. That *śūnyatā* is not a separate absolute but dependent arising itself, the *madhyamā pratipad* articulated without *svabhāva*-reification [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)]. Accordingly, *nirvāṇa* or *mokṣa* cannot be coherently stated as a positive entity, an absence, or a hybrid of the two [[MMK 25.11](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.11)].

translator_note: 'Liberation' is the least bad English gloss, but it easily imports a modern picture of escape, privacy, or merely negative freedom. *Mokṣa* is not one uniform postmortem reward, and it is not always an exit from the world as such. The Sanskrit term carries the metaphysical dispute inside it: whether what is removed is *avidyā*, karmic embodiment, distance from Bhagavān, or contracted self-recognition.
